title: [Program] Local Real-Time Transcription v1 on Sequoia Capture
description:
Umbrella initiative for shipping a fully local, low-latency transcription stack on top of current ScreenCaptureKit capture. This issue anchors goals, sequencing, and decision quality thresholds so implementation remains measurable instead of ad hoc.

## Design
Use the validated capture baseline as the platform foundation, then layer transcription in phases gated by benchmark evidence. Treat latency, reliability, and local-only privacy as first-class requirements, not afterthoughts.

## Success Criteria
Acceptance criteria: 1) A complete dependency graph exists for all major workstreams. 2) Each phase has explicit entry/exit criteria and measurable outputs. 3) Program-level SLOs are represented in child issues and benchmarks. 4) First-run success is prioritized: operators can diagnose environment readiness before recording/transcribing. 5) Transcript outputs are readable and trustworthy by default, with deterministic formatting and clear degradation signaling.

## Notes
This issue is the canonical intent record. Child epics should not drift from local-first privacy, benchmark-driven decisions, and macOS 15+ scope unless this parent is explicitly updated.

title: Phase A: Freeze current runtime contracts and compatibility baseline
description:
Goal:
Freeze the current public/runtime behavior before any modularization or UX work.

Why now:
The project already has external surfaces: CLI flags, runtime mode labels, JSONL control events, manifest fields, trust/degradation codes, Make targets, packaged behavior, and gate scripts. Refactoring before freezing those surfaces would create avoidable ambiguity and regressions.

Definition of done:
We know exactly which current behaviors are compatibility commitments, which are provisional, and which tests will fail if they drift.

comments.author: HoangNB
comments.text: This phase exists to create a factual baseline, not to redesign anything. The objective is to pin down current truth so later work can be additive and safe instead of speculative.

The user-visible definition of near-live in this phase is fixed: `4s` rolling windows with `1s` stride. That cadence is a deliberate tradeoff between responsiveness and compute pressure.

## Scope

- construct rolling windows over the adapted session timeline
- define deterministic segment IDs and ordering
- preserve channel context so later merge/output logic remains intelligible

- overlapping windows should not create non-deterministic event ordering
- the scheduler must be predictable enough for replay and test fixtures
- this is near-live, not full streaming token emission

- Relevant tests or evidence artifacts are added for changed behavior.

title: Productize recordit v1 around human-first and agent-first runtime contracts
description:
Context:
This epic turns the current runtime-centered `recordit` codebase into a human-first and agent-first product surface without throwing away the parts that are already technically strong.

Intent:
Preserve the proven ScreenCaptureKit capture pipeline, bounded queue behavior, lifecycle semantics, trust/degradation reporting, and artifact discipline while making the system substantially easier to operate, easier to extend, and easier for AI agents to reason about.

Why this matters:
Right now the runtime core is strong, but the product surface is still engineer-heavy. The repo exposes many entrypoints, flags, modes, docs, and artifact contracts. That is survivable for maintainers, but it is too expensive for new operators and too ambiguous for downstream automation.

Success criteria:
- one obvious human happy path
- one deterministic machine-readable contract surface
- no accidental behavior drift during refactor/productization
- backward compatibility for existing `transcribe-live` automation and gates
- future contributors can work in modules instead of a monolith

Non-goals:
- inventing a new capture backend
- cross-platform work
- model research or ASR quality re-evaluation in this epic

comments.author: HoangNB
comments.text:
This epic intentionally sequences the work as: freeze contracts -> refactor internals safely -> add new operator CLI -> publish agent-readable schemas/contracts -> simplify human UX -> consolidate docs/workflow -> run compatibility rollout.

That sequence is not arbitrary. The core architectural risk in this repo is not that the runtime is unsound; it is that the runtime is good enough to be depended on, while its contracts are still partially implicit and partly embedded in a very large orchestration file. Freezing the current truth first prevents the refactor and productization work from accidentally changing what downstream users, scripts, and future agents think the system guarantees.

## Background
Model startup/warmup/active/drain/shutdown states explicitly so failure handling, Ctrl-C finalization, and operator-visible readiness remain deterministic.

## Approach

## Constraints
- Preserve representative-mode behavior unless this issue explicitly changes it.
- Keep capture non-blocking under load; degrade ASR work first.
- Prefer additive contract/schema changes so replay and tooling remain compatible.

- Operator-visible output/artifacts make it clear whether the session is warming up, actively streaming, or draining/finalizing.
- Active state is entered only after required runtime readiness conditions are satisfied or an explicit degraded-start policy is surfaced.

The repo currently has reasonable model resolution logic, but it still expects too much operator knowledge. This epic exists to make model configuration deterministic, inspectable, and supportable.

## Why It Matters

For a reliable CLI milestone, model problems are one of the fastest ways to lose user trust:
- explicit model paths that silently fall through are confusing
- missing backend binaries require guesswork
- operators need a "doctor" path that says what is wrong and how to fix it

## Direction

- explicit `--asr-model` must fail fast when wrong
- default setup for whispercpp should be scripted/documented
- manifests should carry enough provenance to explain what the runtime actually used

## Notes
Without this task, downstream performance claims remain anecdotal and cannot be compared reliably.

title: [Epic] Near-Live CLI Productization (Reliable CLI -> Beta Path)
description: Umbrella epic for the next phase of recordit: move from a reliable chained CLI to a truthful, evidence-backed near-live CLI runtime that can later be packaged as a beta.
close_reason: All child epics/tasks are closed and the near-live CLI beta path is now backed by runtime, contract, gate, smoke, and packaging evidence

comments.author: HoangNB
comments.text:
## Background

Recent completed work established a solid baseline for the next phase:
- `bd-491`: one-command capture + transcribe workflow
- `bd-1b8`: transcript + manifest output quality review/rubric
- `bd-bdt` / `bd-2nz` / `bd-2rn` / `bd-4g2`: mixed-rate capture now adapts instead of failing fast

The remaining ambiguity was not whether the project can work, but what the next honest milestone should be.

## Direction Locked In

This phase targets a **reliable CLI** first, not packaging-first polish and not full streaming-ASR research.

"Live" for this milestone means **near-live chunked transcription**:
- one process, not separate daemons/processes
- capture/session worker + ASR worker path
- rolling `4s` windows with `1s` stride
- transcript events printed to the terminal while capture is still running
- full `WAV` + `JSONL` + `manifest` artifacts preserved
- capture must never block on ASR backlog
- overload policy must be explicit: drop oldest queued ASR work and emit degradation/trust signals

## Why This Epic Exists

The repo already proves two important truths:
1. ScreenCaptureKit capture works and can generate usable stereo session WAV output.
2. Whisper-based transcription + event/manifest emission works on representative WAV inputs.

The next product step is to **collapse those truths into one stable operator workflow** that feels live enough to be useful, while preserving the project's strongest traits:
- deterministic artifacts
- explicit telemetry
- measurable regression gates
- trust/degradation signaling instead of silent failure or ambiguous behavior

## Non-Goals For This Phase

- token-by-token or fully streaming ASR UX
- multi-process IPC/file-spool architecture
- packaging-first cleanup before runtime behavior is stable
- hiding degraded behavior instead of surfacing it

## Sequencing Rationale

Recommended order for the entire tree under this epic:
1. build the near-live runtime path
2. harden model provisioning + contract truthfulness
3. add tests and refreshed evidence gates
4. only then consolidate packaging UX

This epic should remain open until the repo can honestly claim:
- near-live CLI emits transcripts during capture
- model/setup experience is deterministic
- output contracts are truthful
- refreshed gates back the claim
- packaging work is a straightforward productization step rather than a moving target

comments.author: HoangNB
comments.text:
## Recommended Kickoff Lanes

To keep work parallel without stepping on unstable decisions, start with **Wave 1** task issues, not the epic containers.

Recommended Wave 1 lanes:
- Live CLI/runtime contract lane: `bd-1qw`
- User-facing terminal UX contract lane: `bd-279`
- Model fail-fast semantics lane: `bd-2pl`
- Model doctor lane: `bd-2lc`
- Whispercpp bootstrap/setup lane: `bd-2g2`
- Artifact contract lane: `bd-2nm`
- Mixed-rate regression evidence lane: `bd-2nh`
- Model provenance/checksum lane: `bd-1g0`

Why this wave exists:
- it locks interfaces and user-facing expectations before deeper implementation fans out
- it gives future agents meaningful parallel work with low file-surface conflict
- it improves the user story early (doctor/setup/truthful output/UX contracts), not only the internals

Operational note:
- `br ready` includes epic containers; for day-to-day task pickup, prefer filtering to executable tasks.

title: [Epic] Artifact Contract Truthfulness and Output Semantics
description: Eliminate mismatches between CLI/docs/manifests and actual runtime artifacts, especially around out-wav and near-live output semantics.
close_reason: All child tasks are closed and acceptance criteria are satisfied by landed gate/runtime/contract evidence

comments.author: HoangNB
comments.text:
## Intent

This epic exists because a CLI that advertises artifacts it does not actually write is not trustworthy. The near-live phase adds more output complexity, so runtime truthfulness must become explicit rather than implied.

## Specific Trust Problem To Solve

`transcribe-live` currently accepts and reports `--out-wav`, but runtime semantics have been ambiguous. That is acceptable in research code, but not for a reliable CLI milestone.

## Decision

`--out-wav` should always refer to the canonical session WAV artifact for the run.
- live mode: full captured session audio
- offline/input-wav mode: a canonical materialized WAV artifact still exists on success

The manifest and docs must match that truth exactly.

title: [Epic] Near-Live Chunk Pipeline in transcribe-live
description: Build the core near-live runtime path inside transcribe-live using one process, rolling chunks, bounded ASR work queues, and explicit degradation semantics.
close_reason: All child tasks are closed and acceptance criteria are satisfied by landed gate/runtime/contract evidence

comments.author: HoangNB
comments.text:
## Intent

This epic is the heart of the next milestone. It converts the current two-step workflow (capture WAV, then transcribe WAV) into a single runtime path that **feels live** while preserving the project's real-time safety rules.

## Fixed Architectural Choices

These choices are already decided and should not be re-litigated during implementation:
- implement inside `transcribe-live`
- one process, not separate capture/transcribe daemons
- no file-spool polling pipeline
- chunking default: `4s` windows with `1s` stride
- capture must never block on ASR lag
- backlog policy: drop oldest queued ASR chunk task and emit explicit degradation/trust telemetry

## Constraints

Implementation must stay compatible with the callback-safety rules already documented in:
- `src/rt_transport.rs`
- `src/bin/sequoia_capture.rs`
- `docs/realtime-contracts.md`

The near-live path should **extend** the existing artifact/event/manifest model, not invent an unrelated second world.
